IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important and practical problem—end-to-end relation extraction—and demonstrates nontrivial empirical gains (±1.9% on ACE05 and 6.8% on CONLL04) by combining joint modeling with globally-normalized beam-search training and parser-hidden-layer augmentation. From my methodology perspective, the focus on global optimization to mitigate label bias and the use of segment (LSTM-Minus) and separate relation/entity LSTMs provides a convincing recipe practitioners could adopt. Limitations around decoding speed with larger beams and reliance on parser hidden states (and no mention of released code) reduce immediate reproducibility, but the approach is likely to influence follow-up work and practical systems in this subfield. | B(4): The paper tackles the central problem of end-to-end relation extraction and does so by combining global training (beam-search with early update) and novel incorporation of parser-hidden LSTM layers into word representations — a meaningful advance over prior table-filling and joint models. Empirical gains (SOTA on ACE05 and large improvement on CONLL04) indicate practical value and suggest practitioners will adopt or extend these ideas. The approach opens new directions around leveraging parser internal states and globally normalized neural decoders for structured IE, though the modest beam-size decoding limitation and reliance on specific parser features keep it from being fully transformative across the broader NLP landscape.

From my methodology perspective, the focus on global optimization to mitigate label bias and the use of segment (LSTM-Minus) and separate relation/entity LSTMs provides a convincing recipe practitioners could adopt. Limitations around decoding speed with larger beams and reliance on parser hidden states (and no mention of released code) reduce immediate reproducibility, but the approach is likely to influence follow-up work and practical systems in this subfield.

The approach opens new directions around leveraging parser internal states and globally normalized neural decoders for structured IE, though the modest beam-size decoding limitation and reliance on specific parser features keep it from being fully transformative across the broader NLP landscape.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear main claims and reports empirical gains on ACE05 and CONLL04, and it describes a plausible technical approach (global beam-training, parser hidden-layer augmentation, LSTM-Minus segments). However, from the provided summary I see gaps in experimental rigor: code/checkpoints are not mentioned, statistical significance testing is not reported, and it is unclear whether strong ablations/control experiments isolate each contribution (e.g., parser hidden layers vs. LSTM-Minus vs. global normalization) or whether baselines were reimplemented under matched settings. Practical limitations (beam-size/decoding speed and parser-error sensitivity) are acknowledged but reduce confidence in generality and deployability. Overall the methods and results look promising but missing reproducibility details and stronger ablation/statistical analysis prevent a higher technical-score. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly articulated and appear supported by experiments showing state-of-the-art gains on ACE05 and CONLL04, and the methodology (global beam-trained table-filling, parser-hidden-layer augmentation, LSTM-Minus segment representations) is technically coherent and grounded in prior work (Andor et al., Dozat & Manning). There are minor technical concerns: no mention of statistical significance testing, beam-size speed limitation (beam=5) that may affect the strength of global optimization claims, and no indication of released code for reproducibility. Overall the experiments and arguments are convincing enough to support the conclusions, with only modest issues that do not overturn the main findings.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well-organized and presents a clear logical flow from motivation through method and experiments, with main contributions stated early (joint end-to-end model, parser-hidden-layer augmentation, LSTM-Minus segments, global optimization). Notation is reported as consistent and hyperparameters are provided, and figures/tables appear effective given the reported clear comparative results on ACE05 and CONLL04. Writing quality is good with thorough related work coverage, though a few practical details (e.g., no mention of released code/checkpoints and the noted beam-size decoding speed tradeoff) could be made more explicit for reproducibility. From a methodological-reviewer perspective the presentation is strong but would benefit from clearer signposting of ablation results and runtime/model-size reporting in the main text. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, including the global optimization and parser-hidden-layer augmentation. Notation and methodological descriptions (multiple LSTMs, LSTM-Minus, beam-search training) are presented clearly enough to understand experiments on ACE05 and CONLL04, and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective. Writing quality and related-work coverage are noted as good/thorough, though some practical caveats (beam-size/decoding speed) suggest a few presentation places could be tightened for clarity and reader guidance.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper composes several existing ideas—globally-normalized beam-search training (Andor et al.), parser hidden LSTM features (Dozat & Manning), LSTM-Minus segment representations, and multi-LSTM table-filling—into an end-to-end relation extraction system. While the specific integration and empirical gains on ACE05/CONLL04 are useful, the core methods are borrowed from prior work and the problem formulation (joint entity+relation extraction) is well-studied, so the contribution is an incremental but non-trivial engineering combination rather than a fundamentally new idea. An expert would find the assembly and empirical validation informative but not surprising. | B(3): The paper integrates several existing ideas—globally normalized beam-search training (Andor et al. 2016), parser hidden-layer augmentation (Dozat & Manning, 2016), and segment representation (LSTM-Minus)—into an end-to-end table-filling relation extraction architecture. Framing joint entity and relation extraction with global optimization and these combined neural modules represents a useful engineering and empirical contribution, but it is largely an incremental synthesis rather than a fundamentally new method given prior joint and globally-normalized sequence models (and closely related end-to-end RE work such as Miwa & Bansal). An expert would find the integration and empirical gains informative, but not surprising as novel theory.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper clearly falls within top-tier NLP/ML scope: end-to-end relation extraction is a core NLP task and the work uses respected datasets (ACE05, CONLL04), standard micro F1 evaluation, and compares to strong prior baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014). Methodologically it uses techniques expected at the venue (bi-affine parser features, LSTM-Minus segment representations, and globally-normalized beam-search training) and reports hyperparameters and limitations, making it relevant to the conference audience. The global optimization angle and reported SOTA improvements make it well positioned for the community, though reproducibility would be strengthened if code/checkpoints were released (not mentioned). | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP research problem (end-to-end relation extraction) and proposes technically relevant innovations (global beam-search optimization, LSTM-Minus segment modeling, and leveraging parser hidden layers) that are directly aimed at the NLP/ML community. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (ACE05, CoNLL04) with established metrics and shows meaningful gains over strong baselines, making it appropriate for top-tier venues. The methods, evaluation standards, and related-work positioning match expectations for the audience, and the reported limitations and empirical analysis are the kind of discussion valued by this community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to several strong, relevant baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016; Li & Ji 2014; Miwa & Sasaki 2014; Gupta et al. 2016) on the standard ACE05 and CONLL04 splits using the same micro-F1 metrics, and it situates itself against global-training (Andor et al.) and parser-based encoding work. However, the summary lacks evidence that baselines were reimplemented or run under identical hyperparameter/seed conditions, no code or checkpoints are reported, and statistical significance/ablation rigor around parser augmentation and global normalization is not documented. Overall the comparisons are good and mostly appropriate, but missing some reproducibility and fairness details that prevent a full top score. | B(5): The paper compares against strong, recent baselines (Miwa and Bansal 2016; Li and Ji 2014; Miwa and Sasaki 2014; Gupta et al. 2016) on the standard ACE05 and CONLL04 splits using the same micro F1 metrics, and reports clear gains. The authors explicitly discuss limitations of prior parsing/tree-LSTM approaches and motivate their design (e.g., parser-hidden-layer augmentation, global optimization), and the related work coverage is described as thorough. Given the available summary, comparisons appear comprehensive and fair with appropriate citation of relevant competing approaches.

However, the summary lacks evidence that baselines were reimplemented or run under identical hyperparameter/seed conditions, no code or checkpoints are reported, and statistical significance/ablation rigor around parser augmentation and global normalization is not documented. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides a solid experimental evaluation on standard benchmarks (ACE05, CONLL04) and reports clear gains over strong prior work, while discussing practical limitations (beam-size decoding speed, parser-error sensitivity) and reporting hyperparameters. Related work appears to be surveyed thoroughly and the global optimization motivation is well-argued. However, the summary does not confirm key reproducibility elements (released code/checkpoints, random seeds, train/dev/test splits, hardware and runtime/latency, model size), and it is unclear whether thorough ablation studies isolate each architectural contribution (parser-hidden augmentations, LSTM-Minus segments, globally-normalized training) and statistical significance testing were performed. Given these gaps—especially around reproducibility and detailed ablations—I judge the experiments as strong but with important minor omissions. | B(4): The paper evaluates on two standard end-to-end benchmarks (ACE05, CONLL04) and reports notable gains versus strong baselines, and it documents practical limitations (beam-size decoding slowdown, parser-error impact) and reports hyperparameters—supporting the main claims. However, the summary does not indicate explicit ablation or sensitivity studies isolating contributions (e.g., parser-hidden augmentations vs. LSTM-Minus vs. global training), which is important for attributing gains. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and limitations are discussed, but reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters given but code not mentioned). Overall the empirical work is solid but missing some analyses needed for complete scientific attribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a solid experimental evaluation on standard benchmarks (ACE05, CONLL04) and reports clear gains over strong prior work, while discussing practical limitations (beam-size decoding speed, parser-error sensitivity) and reporting hyperparameters. However, the summary does not confirm key reproducibility elements (released code/checkpoints, random seeds, train/dev/test splits, hardware and runtime/latency, model size), and it is unclear whether thorough ablation studies isolate each architectural contribution (parser-hidden augmentations, LSTM-Minus segments, globally-normalized training) and statistical significance testing were performed. Given these gaps—especially around reproducibility and detailed ablations—I judge the experiments as strong but with important minor omissions.

However, the summary does not indicate explicit ablation or sensitivity studies isolating contributions (e.g., parser-hidden augmentations vs. LSTM-Minus vs. global training), which is important for attributing gains. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and limitations are discussed, but reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters given but code not mentioned). Overall the empirical work is solid but missing some analyses needed for complete scientific attribution.

main_claims: Introduce a globally optimized neural model for end-to-end relation extraction that jointly performs entity detection and relation classification.
Incorporate syntactic information by augmenting word representations with hidden LSTM layers from a bi-affine attention parser (Dozat & Manning, 2016).
Use segment representations (LSTM-Minus) and separate LSTM-based feature representations for entities and relations to improve performance.
The proposed model achieves state-of-the-art results on ACE05 and CONLL04, improving prior best reported results by 1.9% (ACE05) and 6.8% (CONLL04).
_summary.methodology: Cast relation extraction as an end-to-end table-filling problem using multiple LSTM structures (left-to-right, right-to-left word LSTMs and entity-label LSTM), augment word representations with pretrained parser hidden layers and character/CNN features, model segments via LSTM-Minus, and train with globally normalized beam-search optimization (early-update) as in Andor et al. (2016).
metrics: micro F1-measure (entities and relations exact match)
limitations: Decoding speed becomes intolerably slow when beam size increases beyond 5, so beam size 5 was used.
Parsing errors can lead to encoding inaccuracies of tree-LSTMs, which can hurt relation extraction (motivates using parser hidden layers instead).
Local training suffers from label bias, motivating the use of global optimization.

reviewer_a.name: Methodology Specialist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating sequence tagging and structured prediction models in NLP, with experience reproducing academic results in production codebases. Has led reproducibility audits and implemented globally-normalized and beam-search training regimes in prior projects.
traits: Insists on clear experimental protocols: train/dev/test splits, hyperparameter search details, random seeds, and hardware used
Looks for strong ablations that isolate contributions (e.g., parser features vs. LSTM-Minus vs. global normalization) and checks whether gains persist without heavy engineering
Values rigorous comparisons to properly implemented baselines (re-implemented or matched settings) and statistical significance testing for reported improvements
Attends to reproducibility: requests release of code, model checkpoints, exact pretraining details (parser weights, embeddings), and expects runtime/latency and model size reporting
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical, detail-oriented reviewer who accepts empirical claims only when backed by reproducible experiments, thorough ablations, and clear methodological reporting.

reviewer_b.name: Domain / Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in NLP with a research focus on information extraction and structured prediction, tracking advances in end-to-end relation extraction and the integration of syntactic information into neural models.
traits: Evaluates novelty relative to prior literature: notices prior table-filling and joint entity-relation models and expects clear positioning of the contribution
Values broader impact: how the approach advances understanding (e.g., when syntactic features help), generality across datasets, and practical applicability
Pays attention to significance of empirical gains in context (are improvements large, consistent, or limited to specific settings) and whether the approach changes future research directions

Interested in clarity of writing, intuitiveness of the model design, and whether the proposed techniques (global optimization, LSTM-Minus, parser augmentation) are likely to be adopted by the community
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: High-level, context-aware reviewer who judges the work on novelty, significance, and potential to influence future research agendas in relation extraction.
